metadata.title: Scope Stack: Stop Cramming Everything Into Every Prompt

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Merhaba, Liam — in for Bear. This is Liam, in for Bear? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: This is Liam, in for Bear?
props.segment: Scope Stack: Stop Cramming Everything Into

beats.narration_text: Every Claude session she started with a 400-word preamble: her tone preferences, output format rules, what to avoid, domain context, role framing. By the time she got to the actual task, Claude had processed a document. The prompts felt complete. The outputs felt inconsistent. Preferences stated at the top were ignored by the middle. She was not writing bad prompts. She was putting the wrong things in the wrong place. This is Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: What is the best way to keep my Claude preferences consistent without rewriting them in every prompt?

beats.narration_text: Long prompts are fragile. When instructions crowd out context, Claude weights by recency and structural prominence — which means the task description at the bottom competes with the 400-word preamble at the top. Instructions stated once are not guaranteed to persist across a session. Preferences repeated in every prompt are overhead that costs context. The fix is not a longer prompt. The fix is the right thing at the right level.
beats.new_visual_element: One big prompt fails — long prompts fragile / recency weighting / overhead vs. context / right thing at right level
beats.spark_line: Right thing at the right level.

beats.narration_text: The scope stack has four levels. Account: stable preferences — tone defaults, format preferences, what you always want. Project: recurring workstream context — audience, source constraints, standing instructions. Task prompt: the actual ask — specific, short, with task-level constraints only. Human gate: what you check before the output leaves. Stable at the top, specific at the bottom. Each level adds only what that level needs.
beats.new_visual_element: Four levels — account stable / project recurring / task specific / human gate — top to bottom pyramid
beats.spark_line: Stable at top. Specific at bottom.

beats.narration_text: A project is the organizing unit for recurring workstreams. One project per workstream: the weekly report, the research brief series, the grant application. Project instructions live in the project — persistent context, stable format rules, approved sources. When you start a session for that workstream, the project loads without you rewriting the context. The task prompt shrinks to the actual ask.
beats.new_visual_element: Project as unit — one project per workstream / persistent context / stable instructions / task prompt = actual ask only

beats.narration_text: Memory at the account level is useful for tone and format preferences that never change — and risky for anything that might become stale. Role changed? Project ended? Memory does not know. Review memory monthly. Use it only for preferences that are truly stable and non-sensitive. The scope stack is not a set-and-forget system. It is a structured way to stop rewriting what should already be there.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — scope stack table: account / project / task / gate / each level's content

beats.narration_text: Set up your first project-level context. Paste this.

props.command: I want to set up a project-level context for a recurring workstream. Help me write three sections: (1) Audience and purpose — who reads this and what they need from it, (2) Standing format rules — structure, length, register, what to always avoid, (3) Approved sources — what I may draw on and what I may not. These instructions will apply to every task in this project. I will paste the task-specific ask separately each time.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Scope Stack: Stop Cramming Everything Into Every Prompt] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
